question: Why is regenerative agriculture important?
title: Why Is Regenerative Agriculture Important? The Case for Living Soil | Scotty's Gardening Lab
metaDescription: Regenerative agriculture rebuilds soil health, sequesters carbon, and restores the decay cycle. Learn why it matters — and what's at stake if we keep farming like we have been.
content:
Here's what nobody talks about when they argue about farming: we have spent the last hundred years treating soil like a substrate. A medium. A support structure for the chemicals and synthetic inputs that we pour into it. And in doing so, we've systematically killed the biology that makes soil the most complex and productive ecosystem on the surface of the Earth.

Regenerate means to restore something to a better state. Regenerative agriculture is the recognition that we've degraded something foundational and that we have the knowledge and the tools to bring it back.

I care about this deeply. Not as an abstract policy position, but because I've watched what living soil does when you manage it right. I've seen the difference between dead dirt that needs constant inputs to produce anything and living soil that sustains itself, builds its own fertility, and grows crops that are genuinely more nutritious and resilient.

This is the most important thing I know how to talk about.

---

**The short answer:** Regenerative agriculture matters because healthy soil is foundational to everything — food security, climate stability, water quality, and human nutrition. Conventional agriculture has degraded soil organic matter and biological diversity at a global scale. Regenerative practices — cover crops, reduced tillage, compost application, managed grazing, perennial crops — rebuild soil organic matter, increase biodiversity, and can sequester significant amounts of atmospheric carbon back into the ground.

---

## What We've Done to the Soil

Let me start with the honest assessment of where we are.

Conventional agriculture, as it has been practiced at scale since the mid-twentieth century, is fundamentally extractive. It pulls nutrients from the soil through crops, replaces those nutrients with synthetic fertilizers, and ignores — or actively suppresses — the biological communities that make soil functional. Tillage, herbicides, fungicides, and the general disruption of continuous soil cover have reduced soil organic matter in most agricultural regions of the world to a fraction of its historical levels.

USDA Climate Hubs research documents that agricultural soils have lost 50 to 70 percent of their original soil carbon content over the past century of industrial farming. That's not just a statistic. Each percentage point of lost organic matter represents a corresponding loss in water-holding capacity, in biological diversity, in nutrient cycling capacity, and in the soil's ability to sustain crops without external inputs.

Albert Howard — who I think about constantly when I'm managing my compost piles and thinking about soil — saw this coming a century ago. He watched British agriculture industrialize and wrote passionately about the importance of maintaining what he called the "chain of life" — the continuous cycle of organic matter from living organisms through death and decay and back into fertility. When we break that chain, he argued, we get sick plants, sick animals, and eventually sick people. He wrote this in the 1940s. Every year we understand more clearly how right he was.

## The Decay Cycle: The Foundation of Everything

Here's the concept that I think is most important for understanding regenerative agriculture.

In a healthy ecosystem — a prairie, a forest, a wetland — there is a continuous cycle of organic matter. Living things grow, die, and decompose. Decomposition is driven by an enormous community of organisms: bacteria, fungi, earthworms, beetles, millipedes, nematodes. These organisms break down dead organic matter into simpler compounds, and in doing so, they release nutrients in plant-available forms, build stable humus (long-lasting organic compounds that give soil its characteristic dark color and spongy structure), and create the physical and chemical environment that supports the next generation of growth.

This is the decay cycle. And it's entirely self-sustaining when the inputs are right. No fertilizer bags. No spray programs. No soil tests and application schedules. Just the cycle of life, death, and decomposition feeding the next generation.

Conventional agriculture interrupts this cycle at multiple points. Tillage destroys fungal networks — the mycorrhizal connections between plant roots and soil fungi that allow plants to access nutrients and water from far beyond their root zone. Synthetic nitrogen fertilizers bypass the biological nutrient cycle, providing nitrogen in a form that plants can use directly but that doesn't build long-term soil organic matter. Herbicides that kill everything above the surface also kill a significant portion of the surface-dwelling biology that drives the initial stages of decomposition.

Regenerate agriculture is about restoring that cycle. Giving the biology what it needs to rebuild the organic matter, the fungal networks, the bacterial communities, and the soil structure that make soil productive without constant external inputs.

## What Regenerative Practices Actually Do

Let's get specific about the practices and what the research shows they accomplish.

Cover crops — growing plants specifically to cover bare soil between cash crops — do several things simultaneously. They keep the soil covered, preventing erosion and moisture loss. Their roots create channels in the soil that improve water infiltration. When terminated, they add organic matter to the soil surface. And different cover crop species, particularly legumes, fix atmospheric nitrogen into the soil, reducing the need for synthetic nitrogen inputs.

Reduced tillage or no-till farming preserves the fungal networks that are the connective tissue of soil ecology. A 2021 University of Vermont analysis of regenerative agriculture research found that practices including reduced tillage significantly increased soil carbon storage compared to conventional tillage systems. Oklahoma State University Extension's regenerative agriculture overview confirms that no-till and reduced-till practices are among the most consistently documented soil-health improvements available.

Compost application is the most direct way to restore soil organic matter and soil biology simultaneously. Finished compost contains stable humus compounds, a diverse community of beneficial microorganisms, and bioavailable nutrients. When applied to depleted soil, it jump-starts the biological activity that the soil needs to start sustaining itself.

Managed rotational grazing — moving livestock through paddocks in a pattern that mimics natural herd behavior — has been documented to increase soil carbon and above-ground plant diversity in grassland systems. University of Nebraska research on regenerative farming practices has found that intensive rotational grazing can achieve soil organic carbon increases of up to 3 Mg C per hectare per year in appropriate conditions.

## The Carbon Sequestration Case

One of the major arguments for regenerative agriculture in climate discussions is its potential to sequester atmospheric carbon dioxide in soil organic matter.

Here's how it works. Plants fix atmospheric carbon dioxide through photosynthesis. A portion of that carbon gets deposited in the soil through root exudates, root biomass, and above-ground biomass that decomposes. When soil biology is healthy and soil is not heavily disturbed, a significant portion of that carbon is converted into stable humus compounds that persist in the soil for decades to centuries.

CSU Chico's Center for Regenerative Agriculture and Resilient Systems documents that healthy soil microbiomes are central to this carbon sequestration process — the bacterial and fungal communities in soil actively stabilize organic carbon compounds in forms that resist further decomposition.

American University's research on soil carbon sequestration notes that while regenerative practices show real promise, results vary widely based on climate, soil type, and specific management approach. This is honest. The science is real but the outcomes aren't uniform, and anyone claiming precise carbon numbers for broad adoption should be treated with some skepticism.

What is consistently documented is the direction of effect: regenerative practices build soil organic matter, and conventional practices deplete it. Over time, that difference accumulates.

## The Organic Waste Connection

Here's something I think about a lot that doesn't get enough attention in these conversations.

One of the biggest problems in modern waste management is that our organic waste stream — the kitchen scraps, the yard waste, the food processing residuals — has been contaminated with decay-resistant materials. Plastics, synthetic chemicals, non-biodegradable coatings. Our landfills are full of organic material that would otherwise complete the decay cycle, locked up with plastics that prevent it from breaking down properly.

At scale, the transition to regenerative agriculture requires fixing the organic waste stream. We need to be composting more of our organic waste and returning it to agricultural soil. The waste we generate in a food system should be cycling back into fertility, not accumulating in landfills. This is what the regenerative vision looks like at full scale: the decay cycle restored not just on individual farms but across the entire food system.

I'm not naive about how far we are from that. But I've been building compost piles for years because I believe in contributing to it from where I stand. Every pile of finished compost that goes back into productive soil is one small restoration of the cycle that should never have been broken.

The soil is a living system. It wants to be healthy. Our job is to stop working against it.

---

scottyQuotes: We're worried about what's happening to the weather while we've spent a century killing the biology that holds the soil together and keeps it productive. Regenerative agriculture is about fixing that.

The problem with modern waste management is that our organic waste — the stuff that should be completing the decay cycle — gets mixed with decay-resistant plastics and ends up in landfills instead of back in the soil.
Wood chips are miracle workers in regenerative agriculture. They control weeds, build soil, and create the environment that biology needs to thrive — all without tillage or chemicals.
